headline: GOVT RENEWS SELF-RELIANCE DRIVE, SETS 65% CAP EXPENDITURE FOR INDIGENOUS DEFENCE PRODUCTS
summary: The Indian government is currently renewing its self-reliance drive, with Prime Minister Narendra Modi promoting the 'Vocal for Local' initiative. The government has introduced policies to increase India's self-reliance, including the creation of a new category of procurement and reform of the Ordnance Factory Board. The Ministry of Defence has also decided to spend 65% of its capital expenditure on indigenous products. India is working towards becoming self-reliant in various sectors.

sections.paragraph: India's aspiration for self-reliance, or 'atmanirbharata', is a long-standing goal that dates back to the country's modern history. The concept gained prominence in 2014 when Prime Minister Narendra Modi first used the term in discussions of national security, poverty alleviation, and the Digital India initiative. However, it wasn't until 2020, during the COVID-19 pandemic, that the phrase became a central part of India's economic stimulus package, with a focus on reducing dependence on imports and promoting domestic manufacturing. Despite government initiatives, the manufacturing sector's contribution to GDP growth has declined, and the sector's share of GDP has fallen to historic lows. The government has implemented various policies to increase self-reliance, including the creation of a new category of procurement, Indian Indigenously Designed, Developed, and Manufactured (Indian-IDDM), and allocating a significant portion of the defence budget to domestic procurement. Modi's call for 'Vocal for Local' aims to turn India's quest for self-reliance into a national movement, with a focus on quality and cutting-edge research.

India's economy in 2020 was marked by various challenges, with demand conditions remaining weak, as evidenced by declines in exports and imports. Despite a surge in the S&P BSE Sensex and Nifty50, consumer sentiment remained weak, with the employment outlook being tepid and household incomes sub-optimal. The financial sector also faced issues, with loans turning bad and interest income being hit due to moratoriums and firms folding up. The IMF predicted a 10.3% contraction in the economy in the current fiscal, followed by an 8.8% growth in 2021-22, but warned it may take almost two years for real GDP to recover. The MSME sector, crucial for manufacturing revival, was particularly affected, with many unregistered units not being captured by the Index of Industrial Production. Contact service sectors, such as hotels and restaurants, were also severely impacted and are expected to struggle until the fear of the virus subsides.

sources.title: India's economy in 2020: Year of many questions

sections.paragraph: The geopolitics of the US-China relationship is undergoing a significant shift, with both countries recalibrating their ties. The US remains committed to preventing Chinese hegemony in Asia, but its ability to accommodate India as a strategic counterpoise to China has diminished. Meanwhile, China has been systematically pursuing economic self-reliance for two decades, establishing choke points to pressure the US economy and making it harder for Washington to block China. This shift has implications for India, which faces strategic challenges that will persist beyond leadership changes in the US and China. India must assess trends, anticipate crises, and build indigenous capabilities to navigate this new landscape. The country's salience has declined in both Washington and Beijing, exposing a strategic vulnerability that requires careful management.

China started separating its economy from the west years ...

sections.paragraph: The COVID-19 pandemic has driven changes in market sentiment, with Indian stock indices such as the Nifty 50 nearing pre-pandemic highs. The adoption of digital services and work-from-home arrangements has boosted demand for technology and telecom stocks. Additionally, the self-reliance theme, driven by the government's Atma Nirbhar Bharat initiative, has benefited stocks in the pharma, defence, and manufacturing sectors. Some companies, like those in the food industry, have also seen increased demand due to home consumption. The trend of sourcing from countries outside China has further driven stocks in the pharma and API sectors, as well as defence and electronics manufacturing. Investors are betting on firms that can benefit from import substitution and the global shift in manufacturing away from China.

sources.title: WFH, vaccine, technology and self-reliance drive market sentiment
